title: Searching for cosmic vortices
authors: Marek Nikołajuk, Tomasz Karpiuk, Mirosław Brewczyk
abstract: Our study focuses on the strong tidal disruption of a cold helium white dwarf passing a black hole. We model the white dwarf as a Bose-Fermi droplet and use quantum hydrodynamic equations to simulate the binary system's evolution. As the white dwarf passes through periastron, it loses a significant amount of mass. This mass falls onto the black hole and forms an accretion disc. Quantized vortices appear in the accretion disc, manifesting as strong electromagnetic radiation signals that exhibit characteristic flickering patterns changing on a timescale of a few seconds. Meanwhile, the white dwarf moves away from the black hole. As the white dwarf moves through space, vortices run along its surface. This elongates its geometry, causing it to rotate and emit gravitational waves.
summary: This theoretical study models the tidal disruption of a helium white dwarf by a black hole, treating the matter as a Bose-Fermi droplet governed by quantum hydrodynamics. The key findings are that quantized vortices generate distinct electromagnetic flickering patterns in the resulting accretion disc, and these same vortices cause the escaping white dwarf to emit detectable gravitational waves.
main_problem: The study searches for cosmic vortices generated during the tidal disruption of a cold helium white dwarf passing near a black hole, and investigates the resulting electromagnetic radiation and gravitational wave emission.
main_result: Vortices generate characteristic flickering electromagnetic radiation signals in the accretion disc, and their subsequent propagation along the escaping white dwarf's surface causes measurable gravitational wave emission.
method: The authors use quantum hydrodynamic equations to model the binary system evolution, simulating both electromagnetic power spectra and gravitational wave strain.
model_or_system: The physical system is a cold helium white dwarf modeled as a Bose-Fermi droplet interacting with a black hole. The dynamics are governed by coupled quantum hydrodynamic equations for bosonic and fermionic components using pseudo-Newtonian potentials.
key_observables: Electromagnetic radiation flickering patterns (Power Spectral Density, $P_{	ext{dip}}$), the ratio of bosonic to fermionic dipole power ($P_B^{	ext{dip}}/P_F^{	ext{dip}}$), and gravitational wave strain ($h_+, h_	imes$).
important_parameters: Timescales of a few seconds for flickering; GW frequencies around 1 Hz; BH modeled as non-rotating (Schwarzschild metric).
main_assumptions: The fermionic density follows the bosonic one ($ ar{n}_F =  ar{n}_B$), and the black hole is assumed to be non-rotating.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the process from initial symmetry breaking through vortex dragging into the accretion disc, showing correlations between radiation spikes and vortex presence, and demonstrating the transition of the Power Spectral Density (PSD) from $1/f$ to $1/f^2$.
paper_structure: The paper follows a logical progression: first modeling the initial tidal disruption and vortex generation in the accretion disc (EM signals); second, analyzing the electromagnetic radiation signatures linked to these vortices; and finally, examining the gravitational wave emission from the departing, vortex-laden white dwarf.
why_interesting: This work applies quantum hydrodynamic techniques, typically used for ultracold atomic systems (Bose-Fermi droplets), to extreme astrophysical environments, linking fundamental quantum phenomena (vortices) to observable signals like GWs and EM radiation.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. Bosonic density (left column) and quantum phase (right column) at the z = 0 plane for different time instants. The density and time are in units of 1/a3 B and mBa2 B/ℏ, re- spectively. The positions of the positive and negative quan- tized vortices are marked by red/yellow plus signs and blue minus signs, respectively. Initially, there are no vortices asso- ciated with the white dwarf (top frame), although many are visible in the background noise. Later on, the white dwarf drags some vortices into an accretion disc that is forming (middle frame). Finally (bottom frame), the white dwarf moves away from a black hole, taking a few vortices with it.

Figure 3. Ratio P B dip/P F dip (in red) and the maximum co- variance (in blue) are plotted as a function of time. The red spikes representing the ratio clearly correlate with time intervals of high covariance.

Figure 2. P B dip/P F dip as a function of time. Several peaks are visible within the time interval between 5000 and 10000, indicating the preliminary stage of cosmic vortex formation in the accretion disc. The dipole electromagnetic radiation power is calculated within a cylinder with a radius of 500 aB. Later, the dynamics of the accretion disc become more com- plex.

Figure 4. Electromagnetic dipole radiation power coming from a region with a radius of 700 aB (blue curve) and orig- inating from the ten most powerful plaquettes with vortices (red curve) as a function of time.

Figure 6. Power spectrum density of the radiation coming from the accretion disc is shown as a function of frequency (f). PSD ∼1/f (solid red line) exhibits flicker noise when the preformation period is included in the analysis, followed by a white noise tail at the highest frequencies (top frame). However, when examining radiation from a well-formed ac- cretion disc, the PSD changes qualitatively to PSD ∼1/f 2
